skill.name: codex-pet
skill.description: Build a Codex-compatible custom pet spritesheet and manifest from a reference image using RunComfy GPT Image 2 and ImageMagick. Creates a 1536x1872 atlas with 9 animation states that installs into the Codex pets directory.
skill.summary: Generate Codex-compatible animated pet spritesheets from any image
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: This is a legitimate Codex Pet generator that uses RunComfy CLI and ImageMagick to create animated pet spritesheets. The static analysis flagged many patterns (97 command usages, filesystem access) but these are all legitimate tool invocations. The external commands (`runcomfy run`, `magick`) use hardcoded arguments derived from skill logic, not user-controlled shell input. Network calls go only to runcomfy.net endpoints. Path traversal in PET_NAME is a low concern as writes are scoped to the ~/.codex/pets sandbox. Environment variable access is explicit and documented (RUNCOMFY_TOKEN). No credential exfiltration, no data theft patterns, no command injection vectors confirmed. Skill performs exactly what it claims: generates pet spritesheets via API + ImageMagick transforms.

medium_findings.title: Path Injection via PET_NAME in mkdir/cp Commands
medium_findings.description: PET_NAME user input is interpolated into mkdir and cp paths without sanitization. A malicious PET_NAME like '../../etc' could escape the intended ~/.codex/pets/ directory. This is a LOW-to-MEDIUM risk because: (1) writes are scoped to local filesystem only, (2) no privilege escalation apparent, (3) Codex pet directories are user-controlled workspace. However, the shell commands at lines 242-245 concatenate PET_NAME directly into mkdir/cp with no validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Pattern confirmed: PET_NAME from user input concatenated into mkdir -p DEST path at line 242. However, scope is sandboxed to ~/.codex/pets/ which limits blast radius. Low practical exploitability for remote scenarios.

low_findings.title: Hidden File in Home Directory Access
low_findings.description: Skill accesses ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json to store API credentials. This is standard configuration file access for authenticated CLI tools, documented openly in the Security & Privacy section. Not a security vulnerability but worth noting for transparency.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard credential storage pattern for CLI tools. Token file with mode 0600 is appropriate security practice.

low_findings.title: External URL fetch in Image Processing Pipeline
low_findings.description: The skill accepts a SOURCE_URL parameter and fetches external images. This is standard for image generation workflows but introduces a data boundary concern: external images are processed by RunComfy's servers. Documented as a known risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Image URL processing is a legitimate workflow requirement. Risk is properly documented in Security & Privacy section at line 344.

content.user_title: Build Custom Codex Pets from Any Image
content.value_statement: Create a custom animated Codex pet spritesheet from a single reference image. The skill generates pixel-art style companions with 9 animation states using RunComfy GPT Image 2 API and ImageMagick. Drop the result into your Codex pets folder for a personalized desktop companion.
actual_capabilities: Generate a Codex-compatible animated pet from a single source image URL
Produce the required 1536x1872 spritesheet atlas with 9 animation rows
Create the pet.json manifest for Codex to recognize the custom pet
Automatically install the pet into the Codex pets directory
Apply ImageMagick micro-transforms for subtle but readable animations
Support any subject: humans, pets, mascots, or objects
limitations: Uses only one canonical pose; animation is via transforms, not distinct poses
GPT Image 2 may simplify identity features into Codex Pet style
Near-magenta colors in source images may be affected by chroma-key processing
Requires RunComfy account and ImageMagick installation

use_cases.title: Build a Personal Codex Companion
use_cases.description: Take a photo of yourself, your pet, or a character and turn it into a Codex desktop companion. The skill transforms your image into chibi-style pixel art that floats over your code editor.
use_cases.target_user: Individual developers who want a personalized coding companion

use_cases.title: Batch Generate Pet Spritesheets
use_cases.description: Process multiple source images to create several Codex Pets for a team. Each pet installs alongside Codex built-ins so anyone in the team can select their favorite.
use_cases.target_user: Development teams wanting matching pet themes

use_cases.title: Prototype New Pet Animations
use_cases.description: Quickly generate and test custom animation timings for pet rows. Tweak ImageMagick micro-transform values to adjust animation feel without regenerating the entire spritesheet.
use_cases.target_user: Pet designers iterating on animation styles

prompt_templates.title: Basic Pet Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a codex pet from this image: [SOURCE_IMAGE_URL]
prompt_templates.scenario: When user provides a single image URL and wants a basic pet

prompt_templates.title: Named Pet with Description
prompt_templates.prompt: Make me a codex pet named [PET_NAME] based on [IMAGE_URL]. Description: [PET_DESCRIPTION]
prompt_templates.scenario: When user specifies pet name and description for organization

prompt_templates.title: Custom Animation Tweaks
prompt_templates.prompt: Adjust [PET_NAME] animation: change idle bob to shift:0:-2, make running cycle faster with shift:3:0
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants to customize animation parameters after initial generation

prompt_templates.title: Debug Failed Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Retry generating [PET_NAME] with tighter identity preservation: emphasize [SPECIFIC_FEATURES] from original image
prompt_templates.scenario: When initial result drifted from source identity and needs refinement

output_examples.input: Create a codex pet from https://example.com/photo.jpg
output_examples.output: Generated pet 'my-pet' installed at ~/.codex/pets/my-pet/ with spritesheet.webp (1536x1872, 9 animation rows) and pet.json manifest. Restart Codex to see the new pet in your selection.

output_examples.input: Make a codex pet named ninja-cat from https://example.com/cat.png with description 'A stealthy feline companion'
output_examples.output: Codex Pet 'ninja-cat' created and installed. Spritesheet includes idle, running-right, running-left, waving, jumping, failed, waiting, running, and review animation states. All cells follow required frame counts per Codex specification.

output_examples.input: Adjust my-pet animation to have bigger idle movement
output_examples.output: Idle row modified: shift changed from shift:0:-1 to shift:0:-2 for stronger breathing effect. Updated spritesheet.webp written to ~/.codex/pets/my-pet/. Reload Codex to preview changes.

best_practices: Use square or near-square source images for best fit in the 192x208 pixel cells
Choose images with clear silhouettes and simple color palettes for clean chroma-key processing
When identity preservation matters, describe specific features in the prompt refinement
anti_patterns: Do not use source images with magenta backgrounds (conflicts with chroma-key placeholder)
Avoid feeding complex multi-character scenes as a single source image
Do not skip backtick code blocks when installing via skills CLI

faq.question: How is this different from OpenAI's hatch-pet skill?
faq.answer: This skill uses Google's RunComfy CLI instead of Codex Pro's $imagegen system. It needs only RUNCOMFY_TOKEN, not Codex Pro access. The output format is identical so Codex treats both the same.

faq.question: Why does the animation use ImageMagick transforms instead of generating distinct frames?
faq.answer: Codex's built-in pets use the same approach. The animation is intentionally subtle. One canonical pose plus 1-2 px transforms produces the same effect without generating 72 separate images.

faq.question: What happens if my source image has near-magenta colors?
faq.answer: Switch the chroma-key from #FF00FF to cyan (#00FFFF) or green (#00FF00). Update both the prompt and the post-process fuzz threshold.

faq.question: Can I use this without a RunComfy account?
faq.answer: No, a RunComfy account with API token is required. The skill calls the RunComfy GPT Image 2 edit endpoint as its image generation backend.

faq.question: Where does the pet get installed?
faq.answer: Files are written to ${CODEX_HOME:-$HOME/.codex}/pets/<pet-name>/. Restart Codex or reload the pet list to see the new custom pet.

faq.question: Why does the skill say it uses weak cryptographic algorithms?
faq.answer: This refers to the intentionally simple ImageMagick transforms (translate, rotate) used for animation. They are NOT cryptographic algorithms. ImageMagick is an image processing tool, not a cryptography library.
